You simply can't tell if your kid has real potential to go far until they've gone as far as they can. 

And here's a tale of two ends of it, without using outliers:

1) One on the one end. 60% of high school 5-star recruits make it to the NFL. Ok, so it's a little better than a coin flip, you'll make it *if* you're the best in the country at 18 at your position in high school.

But that's still 4 out of 10 of the BEST 18-year-old high school players who don't make it to the NFL. On the flip side of this same story, on average, about a dozen "0-star" players are drafted each year.

There are currently 257 draft picks, or 4%.

2) Now, for the other piece of data on this. 

A longitudinal study of 47 players at a top Spanish academy used bone-age data to classify biological maturation, with career outcomes tracked 15 years later.  

Late maturers reached professional football at 30.8% vs. 5.6% for early maturers. At follow-up, every player from the cohort competing in a top-5 European league belonged to the late-maturing group. 

So the answer is pretty much you can't tell until you've maxxed out and you're done developing.

People are having interesting reactions to this. Here are my thoughts on why some people don't believe this is enough to improve your running or even replace it.

1) If you've never done 100 true air squats in under 3 minutes, you have no idea how taxing this is on your body. 

Granted, I should put asterisks around the *just* to make it clear that a person untrained in this exercise could see benefit from *just* two sets (whether they actively run or not), but get your body up to doing 4 sets of 100. Then see what you can do. 

2) There seem to be a lot of people who have been taught fitness but never learned the first principles of exercise physiology.

One of the limits to running farther is muscular endurance.  You'll never guess what sets of air squats will do to the muscular endurance of your legs.

Another big limiter—related but not exactly the same—is lactate clearance. 

You'll improve mitochondrial density, capillary density, lactate transport, and your legs' ability to oxidize fat for fuel. 

You also increase the threshold at which lactate begins to accumulate. You'll never guess what all of this means for running. 

3) People have no real experience in sports training. Not academic, but practically playing different sports at a competitive level.

Here's something many don't know about me. I played 3 sports in high school: football, track (100 and 200 m), and swimming.

After my first season of swimming, I went from a 250-lb bench to 275 lb. Without lifting weights. 

Your body adapts specifically AND generally. I'm not saying that I wouldn't have gotten stronger if I had spent that time in the weight room training for the bench. But I am *absolutely* saying that the workout increased my endurance and strength in a way that doesn't seem obvious.

I don’t value material things. 

This is not to say that I don’t think material things are nice to have, but they are fleeting.

They degrade, decompose, and can be destroyed. They carry no memories, but you get angry when you lose them, and much violence can be traced back to people fighting over things.

I have my beef with minimalism, but one thing they absolutely get right is that stuff doesn’t equal happiness.

So what will make you happy?

It’s not that the absence of material objects will make you happy. It’s that focusing on things that endure will bring you the most fulfillment.

Experiences and relationships increase in strength and value over time. Most objects deteriorate and depreciate.

That delicious meal is more enjoyable when you share it with people you care about, regardless of its price or the ambiance of the place you dine in. At the end of the day, all you’re left with is your memories and the memories people have of you–and even then, those will eventually fade.

I focus on experiences, good food, conversation, connection, making a difference, and my craft.
